summary: This lesson extensively practices quedar(se) (to stay) in combination with modal verbs (voy a, quiero, debo, tengo que, puedo) and in the present perfect. The number siete (seven) is introduced. The verb llamar (to call) and 'me llamo' (my name is) are taught, along with cómo (how).

teachings.summary: Extended practice combining quedarse with multiple verb constructions: voy a quedarme, quiero quedarme, debemos quedarnos, tengo que quedarme, puedo quedarme. Also practiced in present perfect: me he quedado, nos hemos quedado, te has quedado.

examples.spanish: Voy a quedarme hasta las siete
examples.english: I'm going to stay until seven o'clock
Do you plan on staying here tonight?

teachings.notes: The reflexive pronoun (me/te/nos) attaches to the infinitive (quedarme) or goes before the changed verb (me he quedado). It is not optional — it's required for the meaning of 'stay'.

teachings.conceptName: Object pronoun position: before changed verb or after infinitive
teachings.summary: Reinforced the precise rule: object pronouns go after the complete verb ('to' form / infinitive) or before the changed verb. Not 'at the beginning' — before the changed verb. This precision matters when sentences have nouns or other words before the verb.
teachings.rule: Object pronouns go: (1) after the infinitive (attached), or (2) before the changed/conjugated verb. Never simplify to 'at the beginning'.

examples.english: We haven't stayed (no before nos, nos before hemos)

teachings.notes: The teacher stresses: if you tell yourself 'at the beginning' instead of 'before the changed verb', you lose clarity when nouns enter the sentence.

teachings.conceptName: Llamar = to call; me llamo = my name is
teachings.summary: Llamar means to call/to phone. 'Me llamo' (I call myself) is how you say 'my name is'. '¿Cómo te llamas?' (how do you call yourself?) = what is your name?
teachings.rule: me llamo = my name is (I call myself). ¿Cómo te llamas? = What is your name? (How do you call yourself?)

examples.english: What is your name? (How do you call yourself?)

teachings.notes: Double-l (ll) pronounced like /y/ in most Spanish-speaking regions.

examples.english: What is your name? (How do you call yourself?)

vocabulary.derivation: September (Septiembre/Setiembre, originally 7th month), e→ie split from 'set'

vocabulary.spanish: Quiero saber por qué tienes que quedarte aquí
vocabulary.english: I want to know why you have to stay here
